channel.title: State-Policy Think-Tank Digest
channel.description: Latest posts from free-market policy institutes across the U.S.

item.title: [Arkansaspolicyfoundation] SOLAR, WIND & ARKANSAS MOTOR VEHICLES
item.description: (February 2026) Arkansans are heavily dependent on private transportation. The Bureau of Transportation Statistics (BTS) reports that 93% of Arkansans reach work by private motor vehicles, while only 0.4% use public transportation. The 102,622 miles of roads in Arkansas dwarf the 2,505 miles of freight railroad and 1,860 miles of waterway cited by the BTS, […]

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Job Opening: Donor Relations and Events Manager
item.description:
Donor Relations and Events Manager Maine Policy Institute (MPI) is seeking an energetic, organized, detail-oriented, full-time development professional to assist our director of operations.  This position will be responsible for accurate donor and database management, including timely donor follow-up and programmatic updates, event planning that engages and educates diverse audiences, coordinating an effective direct mail […]

The post Job Opening: Donor Relations and Events Manager appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] Opinion piece: RGF opinion piece: Medical malpractice success a win, but more needed
item.description:
The following appeared in the Albuquerque Journal on March 2nd, 2026 and in other newspapers across New Mexico. The passage of medical malpractice reform is the most important public policy success in New Mexico in more than a decade. It took support from New Mexicans of every political stripe and a bipartisan coalition in the Legislature to […]

The post Opinion piece: RGF opinion piece: Medical malpractice success a win, but more needed appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] Stephanie Smith: The $100 million myth vs. Alabama’s $12 billion reality
item.description:
Last week, during a "State of the Schools" event in North Alabama, leaders from Huntsville City, Madison City, and Madison County schools sounded a familiar but mathematically creative alarm.

The post Stephanie Smith: The $100 million myth vs. Alabama’s $12 billion reality appeared first on Alabama Policy Institute.

item.title: [Jamesmadison] Building Affordability: The Role of Single-Family Rentals in Expanding Housing Supply
item.description:
The housing affordability crisis is an issue being faced throughout the country.1This has caused frustration for hardworking Americans,...

The post Building Affordability: The Role of Single-Family Rentals in Expanding Housing Supply  appeared first on James Madison Institute.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] LDs 229 + 1089: Higher Taxes. Slower Growth. Fewer Jobs.
item.description:
Politicians in Augusta look poised to pass LDs 229 and 1089 this session, which would raise Maine’s top income tax rate to 10.95%. However, our research concludes that either one of these bills could individually cause serious damage to Maine’s economy if passed. If both bills were passed, it would stack multiple tax increases on […]

The post LDs 229 + 1089: Higher Taxes. Slower Growth. Fewer Jobs. appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Jamesmadison] Levy Caps for Property Tax Reform in Florida
item.description:
Introduction Florida’s current property tax system places a growing financial burden on homeowners, renters, and employers across the...

The post Levy Caps for Property Tax Reform in Florida appeared first on James Madison Institute.

item.title: [Foropportunity] Unleashing Students’ Potential: Georgia Legislators Champion Advanced Math Opportunities
item.description:
Georgia lawmakers have drafted a pair of bills that are set to transform the way math is taught in the state. If the bills become law, they’ll open doors for students to receive a high-quality math education and ultimately shape the direction of their lives.

The post Unleashing Students’ Potential: Georgia Legislators Champion Advanced Math Opportunities appeared first on Georgia Center For Opportunity.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Kansas leads the world in ACT score declines
item.description: The Kansas Department of Education’s mantra – Kansas leads the world in the success of every student – would be more accurately stated as Kansas leads the world in ACT score declines. District-level ACT scores for 2025 were finally made public yesterday, and it’s no surprise that KSDE kept them hidden for months. The state […]

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] Report: “A Review of Metro’s Regional Passenger Rail Futures Study”
item.description:
By Randal O’Toole March 2026 Executive Summary In 2024, the Oregon legislature Senate...

The post Report: “A Review of Metro’s Regional Passenger Rail Futures Study” appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Even Maine’s Democratic Attorney General Argues Ranked-Choice Voting is Unconstitutional
item.description:
Last month, in our Open Letter to Maine’s Legislature, we urged lawmakers to stop advancing LD 1666, the bill that would force ranked-choice voting (RCV) on elections for Governor, State Senator, and State Representative. We reminded lawmakers of the Maine Constitution’s clear plurality requirement, the Maine Supreme Judicial Court’s unanimous 2017 advisory opinion declaring RCV […]

The post Even Maine’s Democratic Attorney General Argues Ranked-Choice Voting is Unconstitutional appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Empowerms] 2026 Capitol Chat: Week 10
item.description:
Capitol Chat: Week 10 At the Mississippi Capitol, nothing is truly dead until it’s dead, dead, dead. This week’s episode features the resurrection of teacher pay raises, movement on pharmacy benefit manager reform, and the latest on a controversial NIL tax proposal.

The post 2026 Capitol Chat: Week 10 first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Beacontn] Fact or Cap? Property Tax Caps in Tennessee
item.description: With so much media coverage and discussion around a property tax cap, what is the reality of such a policy, and is one needed in Tennessee?

item.title: [Thomasjeffersoninst] The Virginia General Assembly Passed Nine Bills to Increase Electricity Costs
item.description:
The 2026 Virginia General Assembly has passed at least nine separate new laws that will increase the cost of your electricity. Not one of the bills creates a single megawatt of additional energy for our use. Most of the bills create new ways for the utilities to take money from all their ratepayers and spend it […]

The post The Virginia General Assembly Passed Nine Bills to Increase Electricity Costs appeared first on Thomas Jefferson Institute for Public Policy.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] Cascade’s Testimony on Metro Spending $50M, 40 Years, Turning Superfund Site to a Park
item.description:
By Naomi Inman Testimony by John A. Charles, Jr. This March, the Metro...

The post Cascade’s Testimony on Metro Spending $50M, 40 Years, Turning Superfund Site to a Park appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF weighs in against proposed Albuquerque gross receipts tax hike in KOAT 7 story
item.description:
RGF has been very active in opposing the plan to enact a massive gross receipts tax hike at Albuquerque City Council. You can find out more about the plan here and even use the links in the post to reach out to your council members. RGF president Paul Gessing recently sat down with KOAT 7 […]

The post RGF weighs in against proposed Albuquerque gross receipts tax hike in KOAT 7 story appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Who Moved in 2025? Nebraska’s Window of Opportunity
item.description:
Every year, the “National Movers Study” from United Van Lines and the “U-Haul Growth Index” provide a real-time heat map of the American economy. The 2025 results are in, and they deliver a clear message: while the mass exodus from high-tax coastal states continues, the “quiet competition” for the middle of the country is heating...

The post Who Moved in 2025? Nebraska’s Window of Opportunity appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] NJ ED REPORT: As Other States Move Forward, Will New Jersey Leave Education Dollars Behind?
item.description:

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] INTERVIEW: NJPI Chairman Discusses the NJ Education Landscape on New Jersey Politics with Laura Jones
item.description:
Check out NJPI Chairman Andy Mulvihill as he is interviewed by Laura Jones on the landscape of K-12 education in […]

The post INTERVIEW: NJPI Chairman Discusses the NJ Education Landscape on New Jersey Politics with Laura Jones first appeared on New Jersey Policy Institute.

item.title: [Empowerms] Rep. Sam Creekmore: Chasing Lions in Healthcare
item.description:
Rep. Sam Creekmore: Chasing Lions in Healthcare In this episode of The Empower Podcast, host Grant Callen sits down with State Representative Sam Creekmore, chairman of the House Public Health and Human Services Committee, for a wide-ranging conversation about the major healthcare issues facing Mississippi. From certificate of need reform and rural hospital access to…

The post Rep. Sam Creekmore: Chasing Lions in Healthcare first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Thomasjeffersoninst] The Schoolhouse as Clinic: The Dangerous Mission Creep of Public School Mental Health Screenings
item.description:
Virginia parents should be alarmed that the Virginia General Assembly just passed House Bill 355  which mandates annual mental health screenings for all public school students in grades 6 through 12. While framed as a compassionate response to a very real “mental health crisis,” it represents a fundamental shift in the mission of public education: from academic instruction to clinical surveillance.  By institutionalizing mandatory mental health screenings, HB 355 threatens […]

The post The Schoolhouse as Clinic: The Dangerous Mission Creep of Public School Mental Health Screenings  appeared first on Thomas Jefferson Institute for Public Policy.

item.title: [Beacontn] The Data Center Panic Isn’t Backed by Data
item.description: Data centers may not be the typical industrial building, like a Nissan, General Motors, or Volkswagen plant, yet they are less of a boogey man than they are made out to be.

item.title: [Will Law] Voters Given Opportunity to Reject Costly 400-Year Veto
item.description:
WILL issued a statement applauding the Wisconsin State Senate for passing Senate Joint Resolution 116. This proposed amendment to the Wisconsin Constitution curbs executive overreach by prohibiting the governor from using the partial veto to create or increase taxes or fees. Voters will have to ratify it this November for it to take effect.

The post Voters Given Opportunity to Reject Costly 400-Year Veto appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] The 2027 Responsible Kansas Budget
item.description: Kansas is relearning a lesson it should already know: the problem is not revenue. The problem is spending discipline. That is the real issue behind the current budget debate. The House budget for fiscal year 2027 would spend nearly $27 billion in total funds and almost $11 billion from the State General Fund, while the […]

item.title: [Inpolicy] Varvel: John Maynard Keynes
item.description: by GARY VARVEL  The First Commandment is clear: “You shall have no other gods before Me” (Exodus 20:3). Obedience to this principle is the foundation of a stable society; conversely, replacing God with any earthly power is a form of idolatry doomed to ultimate failure. While the first five men in this series rejected the God Read the full article...

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] MINNESOTA: Workers heading for the exits
item.description:
For six straight months, more than 100 Minnesota public employees a month have cut ties with their unions.

The post MINNESOTA: Workers heading for the exits appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraskanomics: Senators Brandt and Hallstrom on Property Tax Reform this Year
item.description:
Tune in to the latest episode of Nebraskanomics as we welcome Senator Tom Brandt of District 32 and Senator Bob Hallstrom of District 1. In this episode, the senators join Platte Institute CEO Jim Vokal to discuss their efforts to address Nebraska’s property tax burden through LB 1219 and LB 575 (now amended into LB...

The post Nebraskanomics: Senators Brandt and Hallstrom on Property Tax Reform this Year appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Mspolicy] Dispatch from the Dome: Reform, Regulation, and What Comes Next at the Capitol
item.description:
As the legislative session moves into its final stretch, the focus at the Capitol is beginning to shift. Major policy bills are clearing their final hurdles, others have quietly fallen away, and attention is now turning toward the biggest issue left unresolved: the state budget. Here’s a look at what moved last week — and […]

The post Dispatch from the Dome: Reform, Regulation, and What Comes Next at the Capitol appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Florida Education Association Ordered to Pay Teacher’s Legal Fees After Unfair Labor Practice Ruling
item.description:
Florida’s Public Employees Relations Commission (PERC) has ruled against the Florida Education Association in an unfair labor practice case, ordering the union to cover attorney fees and litigation costs for retired Martin County teacher Kelley LaBedz. Florida’s Public Employees Relations Commission (PERC) has ruled against the Florida Education Association in an unfair labor practice case, ordering the union to cover attorney fees and litigation costs for retired Martin County teacher Kelley LaBedz.

The post Florida Education Association Ordered to Pay Teacher’s Legal Fees After Unfair Labor Practice Ruling  appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Jbartlett] First a paint tax, now a battery tax
item.description:
Gov. Kelly Ayotte on Monday vetoed House Bill 451, which would have established a statewide paint recycling program funded by an “assessment” on paint.  Merriam-Webster notes that for more than 500 years the word “assessment” has meant “to determine the rate or amount of (a tax).” Gov. Ayotte correctly called the assessment a tax, and […]

The post First a paint tax, now a battery tax appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Will Law] WILL Exposes Race-Based Highway Construction Schemes in Four States
item.description:
WILL filed a new Title VI civil rights complaint with the U.S. Department of Transportation (USDOT), identifying four states that are flouting the Trump Administration’s directive to end DEI in highway construction.

The post WILL Exposes Race-Based Highway Construction Schemes in Four States appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Tax Flight Takes Off: IRS Data Reveal Surge of Massachusetts Residents Fleeing to Tax-Friendly States Following the 4 Percent Surtax
item.description:
Net AGI Loss to Florida and New Hampshire Quintuples in a Decade as 26-35-Year-Old Outmigration Continues Newly released 2023 IRS migration data – the first full year after the 4 percent surtax on incomes over $1 million took effect – show Massachusetts losing more than double the amount of adjusted gross income...

The post Tax Flight Takes Off: IRS Data Reveal Surge of Massachusetts Residents Fleeing to Tax-Friendly States Following the 4 Percent Surtax appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.description:

item.description:

item.title: [Thomasjeffersoninst] Governor Spanberger: Don’t Make Principals Jump Through Hoops to Keep Students and Teachers Safe
item.description:
By Ali Ahmad and Derrick A. Max  Conservatives across the country are salivating at the idea that Virginia’s new governor may sign a bill that opens her to the charge that she is siding with school bullies and delinquents over teachers and principals.  House Bill 298, patroned by Delegate Delores McQuinn, is now on Governor Spanberger’s desk. […]

The post Governor Spanberger: Don’t Make Principals Jump Through Hoops to Keep Students and Teachers Safe   appeared first on Thomas Jefferson Institute for Public Policy.

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: The Political Distraction of Youth THERE IS SOMETHING about the middle-aged mind that imagines it can transmit seamlessly to the next generation the observations, wisdom and judgment of a lifetime. The reality, for better or for worse, is that each generation makes up its own mind in its own time. Nonetheless, we continue to launch grand projects in Read the full article...

item.description:
The White House’s National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence is the first serious attempt Washington has made to...

The post The White House Got AI Right. Congress Could Still Get It Wrong. appeared first on James Madison Institute.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Florida’s Private Sector Is Booming
item.description:
When economists assess a state’s economic health, private sector employment is one of the clearest signals of momentum. It’s a lagging economic indicator that corresponds to increases in business creation, wage growth, and consumer activity, so when private sector employment is growing robustly, it bodes well for a state’s overall economic outlook and health.  In recent years, Florida has stood out as one of the...

The post Florida’s Private Sector Is Booming  appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Empowerms] 2026 Capitol Chat: Week 11
item.description:
﻿ Capitol Chat: Week 11 With only a few weeks remaining in the 2026 legislative session, lawmakers are narrowing their focus. This week, we take a look at three issues dominating discussions at the Capitol and what’s on the horizon as the 2026 session winds down.

The post 2026 Capitol Chat: Week 11 first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.description:
The power to make laws should rest with the people and their elected representatives—not government bureaucrats who impose costly rules..

item.title: [Will Law] WILL: Evers Veto of “Red Tape Reset” is Irresponsible
item.description:
Governor Tony Evers vetoed the WILL-backed “Red Tape Reset,” a comprehensive regulatory reform package supported by a broad coalition of free-market groups and Wisconsin job creators.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] LAUSD is broke, and the union knows it
item.description:
After Los Angeles Unified delivered 3,200 pink slips to teachers and employees last month, the United Teachers Los Angeles has the temerity to strike for higher pay. As one of the most fiscally distressed school districts in America, drowning in an $11.4 billion unrestricted net deficit, hemorrhaging students, and still without its own superintendent due to ongoing...

The post LAUSD is broke, and the union knows it first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] IRS domestic migration data shows another net loss for Kansas and Johnson County
item.description: The 2023 domestic migration data released last week by the Internal Revenue Service shows that Kansas lost $361 million in adjusted gross income (AGI) from U.S. residents moving in and out of the state. That is a smaller loss than in 2022, but more than was lost in 2020 and 2021. Johnson County elected officials […]

item.description:

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Rethinking California’s Safety Net: A Call for True Compassion
item.description:
Imagine ten regular adults pulling a large, empty cart along a flat road. As one participant is injured, the group loads the person into the cart and, with relative ease, continues on their journey. As a second and third person become incapacitated or are no longer able to contribute in a meaningful way, they are...

The post Rethinking California’s Safety Net: A Call for True Compassion first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] Spending Control Equals Property Tax Relief
item.description:
Property taxes are rising faster than incomes: In both Iowa and Texas, collections have surged well beyond inflation and population growth, putting increasing pressure on homeowners and businesses. The core driver is spending growth: As local government budgets expand, property tax bills follow—making spending restraint central to any long-term solution. Lasting relief requires structural reform: Short-term fixes like credits or rebates help temporarily, but aligning spending growth with economic realities is key to making property taxes more predictable and sustainable.

The post Spending Control Equals Property Tax Relief appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] You Should Break Up with Your AI Boyfriend
item.description:
You should break up with your boyfriend. Not your real, human boyfriend—your AI boyfriend. Recent media coverage and cultural moments have brought renewed attention to how people are forming relationships with artificial intelligence, and how we ought to think about these rapidly developing technologies. A recent Prime Video series, “Scarpetta,” offers a striking fictional example....

The post You Should Break Up with Your AI Boyfriend first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Yankeeinstitute] What Stanley Black & Decker’s Plant Closure Says About CT’s Economy
item.description:

item.description: The Elevation Award is MSPC’s highest honor, awarded to individuals whose work has elevated public policy, strengthened communities, and inspired principled leadership across the region and nation.

item.description:
JACKSON, MS — The Mississippi Center for Public Policy (MCPP) hosted British author and science writer Matt Ridley for a special luncheon on Thursday, March 19, bringing together policymakers, business leaders, and community members for a discussion on energy, innovation, and economic growth. Ridley delivered an optimistic, data-driven presentation highlighting the critical role of free […]

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Louisiana AI Legislative Update
item.description: The Louisiana Regular Legislative Session convened on March 9th. Lawmakers introduced more than 20 bills related to artificial intelligence (AI) for consideration. This focus on legislating AI is in line with national trends, as regulatory proposals multiply in states across the country. Over recent years, the economic and political forces of Louisiana have been unable to avoid...

item.title: [Jbartlett] Massachusetts lost 145,000 residents to New Hampshire over the last 20 years
item.description:
Massachusetts lost an estimated 145,000 residents to New Hampshire over the last 20 years, a Josiah Bartlett Center analysis of U.S. Census migration data shows. For scale, that number of people living in one place would be Massachusetts’ fourth-largest city, larger than Cambridge and slightly smaller than Springfield.  The U.S. Census Bureau estimates that 369,368 […]

The post Massachusetts lost 145,000 residents to New Hampshire over the last 20 years appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Jbartlett] NH ranks first in taxpayer ROI for 11th straight year
item.description:
New Hampshire ranked No. 1 in taxpayer return on investment (ROI) for the 11th year in a row, according to financial services website WalletHub.  “New Hampshire is the state with the best taxpayer return on investment, which is due in large part to the fact that it has no state income tax,” WalletHub’s report concludes. […]

The post NH ranks first in taxpayer ROI for 11th straight year appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.description: Let’s avoid the awkward situation of explaining to Idaho families why a program designed to help them ended up putting them on hold.

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Congressional Affordability Fix Must Hold Hospitals Accountable
item.description:
The nation’s top hospital lobbyist testified before the House last week for the third in a series of hearings on healthcare affordability. According to a recent poll from KFF (f/k/a Kaiser Family Foundation), a little less than half of Americans say they have trouble affording healthcare. Over one-third have skipped or delayed needed treatment in […]

The post Congressional Affordability Fix Must Hold Hospitals Accountable appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.description:

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] Alabama poised to make dramatic change to primary voting. The question is – who foots the bill?
item.description:

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] The Economics of Managing Mono Lake
item.description:
Along with the California Condor, one of our state’s most magnificent environmental success stories of the 20th century is how Mono Lake was saved. In the early 1980s, after decades of unsustainable water withdrawals from the Owens River into the Los Angeles Aqueduct, the lake had declined in elevation to 6,372 feet. The decline was...

The post The Economics of Managing Mono Lake first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Farming, actually … is all around
item.description:
There is this movie – either you love it or you hate it. That is how I feel about National Agriculture Month. At the beginning of that movie, though, there is a monologue that is pretty good and ruminates on the meaning of, well, love, but it works for food production, too … if you’re […]

item.description:
Tune in to the latest episode of Nebraskanomics as we welcome Nebraska State Treasurer Joey Spellerberg. During the episode Treasurer Spellerberg and Platte CEO Jim Vokal discuss LB 938, which creates First-Time Home Buyer Savings Accounts in Nebraska, the role of the Treasurer’s Office, and solutions for Nebraska’s high property taxes. Listen now on Apple...

The post Nebraskanomics: Treasurer Joey Spellerberg on First-Time Home Buyer Savings Accounts in Nebraska appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Mspolicy] Dispatch from the Dome: A Step Forward on Healthcare Reform in Mississippi
item.description:
After a session filled with difficult setbacks and stalled reforms, this week brought a meaningful — and important — step forward. On March 23, HB 1622 was signed into law by the Governor, marking another shift in how Mississippi approaches healthcare regulation. While it is not full repeal of Certificate of Need (CON) laws, it […]

The post Dispatch from the Dome: A Step Forward on Healthcare Reform in Mississippi appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] ITR Brings Property Tax Clarity to Rockwell City
item.description:
A crowd of engaged residents gathered in Rockwell City last week for a conversation on one of Iowa’s most pressing issues: property taxes. Hosted by local community members, the event featured ITR Foundation Research Director Sarah Curry, who provided a clear, practical walkthrough of how Iowa’s property tax system works and how taxpayers can better understand what’s happening in their own communities. The discussion quickly became highly interactive, with attendees, including local elected officials, eager to dig into the details behind their property tax bills. Sarah explained the fundamentals of how property taxes are calculated, what drives increases, and why headline claims about “rate reductions” don’t always tell the full story.

The post ITR Brings Property Tax Clarity to Rockwell City appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Louisiana Can’t Afford More Dedicated Funds in the State’s Budget
item.description: Louisiana lawmakers continue to make the state budget more rigid and less responsive by expanding the use of dedicated funds—an approach that undermines fiscal responsibility and limits the Legislature’s ability to fund core priorities. While dedicating revenue streams to specific purposes may sound prudent, the reality is that Louisiana’s overreliance on these mechanisms has created...

item.title: [Empirecenter] New York Still Hasn’t Learned from its Covid Mistakes
item.description:
Today marks the sixth anniversary of a fateful moment in New York’s coronavirus pandemic: the Cuomo administration’s order sending Covid-infected....

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Focus on transit: How to save BART without raising taxes

While I question how “beloved” BART is to anyone other […]

item.title: [Johnlocke] Holding Jack Smith and his lawfare team accountable
item.description:
Margot Cleveland of the Federalist offered Congressional testimony about significant federal government misdeeds. After the 2020 election, an anti-Trump FBI agent named Tim Thibault attempted to use the justice department to destroy the President. Thibault’s efforts led to the launch of Arctic Frost. Soon after, Merrick Garland tapped Jack Smith, a “hyper-aggressive prosecutor,” known to “overstretch...

The post Holding Jack Smith and his lawfare team accountable appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Johnlocke] California school district accused of giving phony degrees to Chinese students
item.description:
Preston Mizell writes for FoxNews.com about a disturbing revelation involving one California school district. An extensive audit revealed a California school district allegedly issued diplomas to students in China in addition to potentially “unlawful financial practices associated with the district’s international program.” The Val Verde Unified School District (VVUSD) in Riverside, California, was audited by...

The post California school district accused of giving phony degrees to Chinese students appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Mackinac] The Economic Spirit of 1776
item.description: Political freedom and economic freedom: a healthy combination

item.title: [Johnlocke] Solar’s cost should reflect its need for backup generation
item.description:
Imagine a car without an engine. It doesn’t need expensive gasoline. It can reach fairly high speeds — if it’s going down a steep and long enough grade. Now imagine buying this car without an engine, hoping for nature to provide enough downhill descents, and boasting of having no fuel costs. Then imagine wanting to...

The post Solar’s cost should reflect its need for backup generation appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Illinois home prices jump as inventory plunges
item.description:
The state’s housing inventory is at just 31% of pre-pandemic levels, driving up prices. Restrictive zoning and the nation’s highest property taxes must be addressed.

The post Illinois home prices jump as inventory plunges appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Meet the Candidates Running for Louisiana’s Public Service Commission
item.description: When Louisiana voters go to the polls this May, most of their attention will be fixed on the U.S. Senate race. But tucked into the same ballot sit two contests that may ultimately do more to shape the financial lives of ordinary Louisianans than anything happening in Washington.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] Who Controls Property Taxes in Iowa? Understanding the State–Local Balance
item.description:
Local governments in Iowa do not have unlimited authority over property taxes, they can only levy taxes as authorized by the state and must operate within state law. Home Rule provides flexibility in managing local affairs, but the legislature retains ultimate control, especially over taxation. The current debate over property tax reform is ultimately about priorities: ensuring essential services are funded while encouraging local governments to focus spending on core responsibilities before expanding into nonessential programs.

The post Who Controls Property Taxes in Iowa? Understanding the State–Local Balance appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Poll: Nine out of Ten in Hampden County Support Expanding Access to Vocational-Technical High Schools
item.description:
BOSTON – A new poll commissioned by Pioneer Institute finds that an overwhelming 90 percent of Hampden County residents support expanding county students’ access to vocational-technical high schools.   “Vocational-technical high schools are a bright spot amid an extended period of declining...

The post Poll: Nine out of Ten in Hampden County Support Expanding Access to Vocational-Technical High Schools appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.description:

Wisconsin […]

The post From the Heartland Post: New IRG Study Shows Exactly How to Win Wisconsin’s Gubernatorial Race appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] Republican bill to reverse DFL’s car tab hike would have helped Minnesotans with affordability
item.description:
How the DFL “trifecta” made Minnesota less affordable One of the ways the DFL’s “historic” trifecta of 2023 hit Minnesotans in the pocket was by hiking car tab fees. This hike has generated some recent comment. Yesterday, CBS News reported: If you have a newer car, you may be in for some sticker shock when...

The post Republican bill to reverse DFL’s car tab hike would have helped Minnesotans with affordability appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Taxes are High. Local Spending is to Blame.
item.description:
Over the last few months, local property taxes have been described as: “steep,” “burdensome,” “stressful,” “too high,” and “out-of-control.”   Yet despite these sharp (and accurate!) criticisms of Texas’ property tax, many local governments seem oblivious to the system’s excesses, as evidenced by the adoption of high tax rates this year (see TPPF’s Taxman Cometh series) as well as historical tax, spending, and debt patterns. Worse, what once may have been excused as indifference or detachment now borders on exploitation.   Consider some recent revelations about local government compensation.  ...

The post Taxes are High. Local Spending is to Blame. first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] Easing ADU rules in Maui County could expand housing options
item.description:
The following commentary was first published on March 22, 2026, in Maui Now under the headline “Proposed ADU rules could help boost Maui County housing supply.”_____________ Maui County homeowners could soon have more freedom to build accessory dwellings on their properties if two recent proposals become law. One, Bill 27 (2026), would authorize ADUs to be slightly […]

The post Easing ADU rules in Maui County could expand housing options appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] The federal machine-gun ban rests on a dangerous constitutional theory
item.description:
If possession alone counts as interstate commerce, Congress can regulate almost anything. Think back to fourth-grade American history. We learned why the Articles of Confederation failed and why the Constitution replaced them. One major problem was that states struggled to trade with one another and often tried to protect local interests by taxing or restricting...

The post The federal machine-gun ban rests on a dangerous constitutional theory first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] Grade inflation and its impacts on students
item.description:
There are many different measures used to determine how a student is doing academically — teacher observations, in-class assignments, quizzes and tests, report cards, benchmark exams, and standardized assessments, to name a few. But when those measures send conflicting signals, it can be unclear whether students are actually learning and prepared for the next step....

The post Grade inflation and its impacts on students appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] Building the foundation for attainable housing in Idaho
item.description: Idaho homebuyers have few attainable choices in the existing marketplace. These proposed policies deregulate the housing restrictions that are barring new entrants, creating more attainable choices in the marketplace.

item.description: "This awesome victory will help music made by people, together, in Music City, keeping creativity alive.”

Tax Day is around the corner, and a new national report suggests Connecticut taxpayers are getting less for their money — and falling further behind.  According to WalletHub’s 2026 “States […]

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Senate Bill 1430 — Firearms regulations, preemption (+3)
item.description: Bill Description: Senate Bill 1430 would expand state preemption of local firearm regulations, provide for penalties against political subdivisions, and provide legal recourse for those harmed by local regulations of firearms. Rating: +3 Does it violate the spirit or the letter of either the United States Constitution or the Idaho Constitution? Examples include restrictions on speech, […]

item.title: [Idahofreedom] House Bill 944 — Liquor account, distribution (-1)
item.description: Bill Description: House Bill 944 would transfer $5 million annually from the state’s Liquor Account to the Idaho Law Enforcement Fund. Rating: -1 NOTE: Senate Bill 1360 (2026) and Senate Bill 1379 (2026) proposed increasing vehicle registration fees for the same purpose that this bill would authorize a recurring fund transfer. Does it in any way […]

item.title: [Idahofreedom] House Bill 946 — Litigation financing transparency (-4)
item.description: Bill Description: House Bill 946 would regulate and restrict what it defines as third-party litigation financing, effectively limiting the right of contract for both litigants and litigation financiers. Rating: -4 NOTE: House Bill 946 is related to House Bill 646 (2026).  Does it create, expand, or enlarge any agency, board, program, function, or activity of government? […]

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] Hair-braiding bills hint at deeper issues with occupational licensing
item.description:
Two bills making their way through the Hawaii Legislature would relieve natural hair braiders of the onerous requirement that they obtain full cosmetology licenses to practice their craft professionally.  Mandating expensive cosmetology schooling for braiders, whose skills often have a rich cultural history of being passed down for generations, is not only unnecessary — it’s […]

The post Hair-braiding bills hint at deeper issues with occupational licensing appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.

item.description:

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: The Democratic Illusion “NO KINGS” PROTESTS are gearing up in Indiana for this weekend. It is a good time to review the state of democracy. It’s in bad shape, obviously. We don’t seem able to agree on even whether elections should be “fair.” Nobody, or very few of us, would dare defend the alternative, monarchy. That’s why Read the full article...

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] Kent says cut spending, not tax relief
item.description:
The state doesn’t have a budget crisis. It has a spending crisis. That’s what Grassroot Institute of Hawaii Executive Vice President Joe Kent told KHVH News Radio 830 host Rick Hamada during “The Rick Hamada Program” on March 16. Kent said the state’s latest budget woes are “less about the federal dollars stopping and more […]

The post Kent says cut spending, not tax relief appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.
